reasoning: All three critical semantic correctness bugs must be fixed before performance optimization can be effective. Iteration 1 regressed despite parallelism improvements (1.57s vs 3.25s) because the underlying date encoding mismatch and wrong-results issue were not addressed. With only 1 iteration remaining and correctness as the blocking prerequisite, continuing performance optimization would be counterproductive. The system needs a solid correctness foundation before pursuing parallelism.
focus_areas: Fix DATE_CUTOFF year-only vs full-date comparison logic
Fix output format to convert o_orderdate integer to YYYY-MM-DD string
Debug join pipeline to verify cardinality and fix wrong-results bug
strategy_notes: The next iteration should focus EXCLUSIVELY on the three critical fixes identified in iter_1_evaluation.json. Do not attempt performance optimization (parallelism, hash tables, SIMD) until validation passes. Iteration 1's attempt to add parallelism before fixing semantics was the right instinct (it yielded 2x speedup), but it failed validation and was rolled back. The rollback mechanism works correctly. Recommend: (1) Fix date encoding in query code to handle year-only values correctly, (2) Implement proper date string formatting, (3) Add debug output to verify intermediate join cardinalities match expected values, (4) Revalidate against ground truth. Only after correctness is achieved should iteration 3 (if available) be used for performance optimization.
notes: Critical insight from iteration history: Semantic correctness is a hard prerequisite. Parallelization and other performance optimizations cannot salvage incorrect results. Iteration 1 showed that parallelism DOES work (3.25s → 1.57s) but without correctness, the speedup is meaningless. The final iteration must prioritize correctness over performance. All three critical fixes in iter_0_evaluation.json::critical_fixes must be addressed.
